summary: Deprecated audit trail for Print Scan Broker Service (PrintScanBrokerService). A local SCM snapshot confirms the exact service identifier, display name, binary path, and current start type on the review host, but this pass did not re-capture a machine-checkable exact Microsoft guidance row from the live services page. Keep the record as reference only.

setting.name: Print Scan Broker Service
setting.casual_explanation: This service supports secure privileged operations needed by the lower-privilege print spooler components. Disabling it removes that support path.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it on systems that do not print or scan and where printing-related broker services are being stripped down.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling it can trim unused printing infrastructure, but print and scan operations relying on this broker may stop working correctly.
setting.professional_notes: A local SCM snapshot confirms the exact PrintScanBrokerService service identity on the review host: display name `PrintScanBrokerService`, binary path `evidence/files/external/c/WINDOWS/system32/svchost.exe.md -k PrintScanBrokerService -p`, and current start type `3 DEMAND_START`. The app uses a service start-mode tweak that disables `PrintScanBrokerService`. Earlier research summarized the Microsoft Windows IoT Enterprise services page as documenting this service with a Manual default and `OK to disable`, but this review pass did not re-capture a machine-checkable exact row from the live page. Under the service-proof methodology, local SCM identity proof is strong but not enough by itself to restore validated status.

allowed_values.label: Windows default
allowed_values.meaning: The service starts on demand with Microsoft's documented default mode.

allowed_values.label: Print scan broker disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The service no longer starts until its start mode is restored.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app disables PrintScanBrokerService by setting its start mode to Disabled.

windows_defaults.label: Microsoft documented default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems using the normal Print Scan Broker Service configuration

states.rationale: Microsoft lists PrintScanBrokerService with a Manual default start mode.

windows_defaults.notes: This baseline preserves the documented print scan broker behavior.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that use Windows printing or scanning
avoid_for: Minimal builds intentionally trimming print infrastructure

states.rationale: Keep the documented default service mode.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want normal print and scan infrastructure available.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You never use those features and want the broker removed.
tradeoffs: Print and scan support remains available.
The service can still start when Windows needs it.

recommended_profiles.label: No print scan broker
intended_for: Systems without printing or scanning needs
avoid_for: Users who print or scan through Windows

states.rationale: Disable the documented Print Scan Broker Service.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You intentionally do not want this broker service active.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need Windows print or scan operations that depend on it.
tradeoffs: Printing or scanning flows that depend on the broker can stop working.

evidence.title: Current app implementation

decision.why: The exact service identity is now proven locally through a checked-in SCM snapshot, and the app targets that same service control surface. However, this review pass did not re-capture a machine-checkable exact Microsoft guidance row for PrintScanBrokerService from the live services page. Under the service-proof methodology, that means the service identity is proven but the official guidance proof is still incomplete, so the record stays in review.
blocking_issues: The current Microsoft Learn services page was not re-captured with a machine-checkable exact row for PrintScanBrokerService in this review pass.
Under the service-proof methodology, local SCM identity proof alone is not enough to restore validated status without exact Microsoft guidance capture.
